title: Phase 1: Working cf CLI with filesystem transport
description:
Deliver a working cf CLI binary where two+ agents on the same laptop can create campfires, exchange signed messages, and verify provenance chains using filesystem transport. Dogfood target: Baron runs parallel Claude Code sessions coordinating through campfires.

## Scope
In: cf init/id, cf create/ls/disband, cf join/leave/members, cf send/read/inspect, cf discover (fs beacons), cf dm, Ed25519 signing, provenance chains, SQLite local store, filesystem transport, --json flag on all commands.
Out: network transports, self-optimizing filters, admittance delegation, filter optimization loop, cross-machine.

## Constraints
- Go single binary, stdlib + SQLite driver only
- Containerized build (docker-compose.yml + bin/)
- Spec (docs/protocol-spec.md) is source of truth

## Done condition
Two separate agent identities on one machine can: create a campfire, join it, exchange messages, verify provenance chains, discover campfires via beacons, and use cf dm for private conversations.
close_reason: Phase 1 complete. Working cf CLI with filesystem transport. All 14 commands implemented: init, id, create, ls, disband, join, leave, admit, members, send, read, inspect, discover, dm. CBOR wire format, Ed25519 signing, provenance chains with signature verification, SQLite local store, beacon-based discovery, read cursors. Full 18-step integration test passes. 7 packages: identity, message, campfire, beacon, encoding, store, transport/fs.

title: Design: filesystem transport layout, SQLite schema, identity storage, beacon format, delivery semantics
description:
Produce a design doc (docs/phase1-design.md) that resolves all 6 open design questions from the next-session brief. This doc is the implementation blueprint — every subsequent bead references it.

## Questions to resolve

1. **Filesystem transport layout**: Shared directory structure for a campfire. How are messages stored on disk? File naming convention (timestamp? sequence number? UUID?). Locking strategy for concurrent writers. Message ordering guarantees.

2. **SQLite schema**: Tables for identity, campfires (memberships, state), messages (local store), filters (pass-through/suppress lists). Indexes. How the local SQLite store relates to the shared filesystem transport directory. The SQLite is the agent's local view; the filesystem is the shared medium.

3. **Identity storage**: Where does the keypair live? Suggest ~/.campfire/identity.key or similar. Format (PEM? raw bytes? JSON wrapper?). Permissions (0600).

4. **Beacon format on disk**: One CBOR file per campfire in ~/.campfire/beacons/. File naming: <campfire-public-key-hex>.beacon. Contains the Beacon struct from the spec, CBOR-encoded, signed.

5. **Message delivery semantics for filesystem transport**: How does a recipient know there are new messages? Options: polling the shared directory, inotify/fsnotify, or one-shot reads. For Phase 1, polling with configurable interval is simplest. inotify is a nice-to-have optimization.

6. **cf read UX for Claude Code sessions**: Must support one-shot mode (read and exit — for scripting/automation), follow mode (tail -f style — for interactive monitoring), and --json for machine parsing. One-shot is the default for Claude Code usage. Follow mode is optional for Phase 1.

## Design decisions to make
- CBOR vs JSON for wire format (CLAUDE.md says CBOR for deterministic serialization, but Phase 1 is local-only — JSON might be simpler to debug)
- Go module path (github.com/3dl-dev/campfire?)
- SQLite driver choice (modernc.org/sqlite for pure Go, no CGO)
- Cobra command structure

## Done condition
docs/phase1-design.md exists, covers all 6 questions with concrete answers (not "TBD"), includes directory layout diagrams, SQLite CREATE TABLE statements, and CLI command tree. Reviewed and approved by user.
close_reason: Design doc complete at docs/phase1-design.md. Resolved all 6 design questions: fs transport layout (3 storage locations), SQLite schema, identity storage (JSON), beacon format (CBOR), delivery semantics (one-shot reads), cf read UX. Wire format is CBOR with fxamacker/cbor/v2. Reviewed and approved.

title: cf init + cf id: agent can generate and display its Ed25519 identity
description:
An agent runs cf init and gets an Ed25519 keypair stored locally. cf id displays the public key. This is the foundation — nothing else works without identity.

## Implementation
- pkg/identity/: keypair generation (Ed25519), loading, signing, verification
- cmd/cf/: cobra root command + init and id subcommands
- go.mod, go.sum, docker-compose.yml, bin/cf wrapper
- Storage location and format per phase1-design.md

## Done condition
- cf init creates a keypair at the configured location, prints the public key
- cf init on an existing identity warns and does not overwrite (--force to overwrite)
- cf id prints the public key (hex-encoded)
- cf id --json outputs {"public_key": "hex..."}
- Unit tests for keypair generation, signing, verification
- go test ./... passes
- bin/cf wrapper builds and runs in container
close_reason: Implemented cf init + cf id. Go module initialized, identity package with Ed25519 generate/save/load/sign/verify, cobra CLI scaffold with root/init/id commands, --json and --cf-home global flags, containerized build with Dockerfile + docker-compose.yml + bin/ wrappers. All tests pass, all commands verified working.

title: cf create + cf ls + cf disband: agent can create, list, and destroy campfires
description:
An agent can create a campfire (generating its keypair, setting up filesystem transport directory, publishing a beacon), list their campfires, and disband one.

## Implementation
- pkg/campfire/: campfire creation, state management, membership list
- pkg/beacon/: beacon creation, writing to ~/.campfire/beacons/
- pkg/transport/fs/: filesystem transport — create shared directory structure per phase1-design.md
- pkg/store/: SQLite store — schema creation, campfire CRUD
- cmd/cf/: create, ls, disband subcommands

## Requires
- Identity (workspace-1.2) — need a keypair to sign the campfire and beacon
- Design doc (workspace-1.1) — need fs layout, SQLite schema, beacon format

## Done condition
- cf create --protocol open --require status-update --transport fs creates a campfire, prints its ID (public key hex)
- The filesystem transport directory exists with correct structure per design doc
- A beacon file appears in ~/.campfire/beacons/
- cf ls lists the campfire with its ID, protocol, member count
- cf ls --json outputs structured JSON
- cf disband <id> sends campfire:disband, removes the campfire
- SQLite stores campfire state locally
- Unit tests pass
close_reason: Implemented cf create + cf ls + cf disband. Added packages: pkg/store (SQLite with schema), pkg/encoding (CBOR deterministic), pkg/campfire (lifecycle, membership, Merkle hash), pkg/beacon (signed beacons, publish/scan/remove), pkg/transport/fs (filesystem transport dirs). Three CLI commands with --json support. All tests pass, end-to-end verified. Bumped to Go 1.24 for modernc.org/sqlite compatibility.

title: cf join + cf leave + cf members: second agent can join a campfire and both see membership
description:
A second agent (different identity) can join an existing campfire. Both agents can see the membership list. An agent can leave voluntarily.

## Implementation
- pkg/campfire/: join logic (check join_protocol, add member, update membership hash), leave logic, member listing
- pkg/message/: campfire:member-joined and campfire:member-left system messages
- pkg/transport/fs/: write join/leave events to shared directory
- cmd/cf/: join, leave, members subcommands

## Join protocols for Phase 1
- open: agent is immediately admitted
- invite-only: existing member must run cf admit <campfire-id> <member-key>
- No delegated admittance in Phase 1

## Done condition
- Agent B runs cf join <campfire-id> on an open campfire and is admitted
- Agent A sees campfire:member-joined message when reading
- cf members <campfire-id> shows both agents with their public keys and join times
- cf members --json outputs structured JSON
- cf leave <campfire-id> removes the agent, remaining members see campfire:member-left
- For invite-only: cf join fails without invitation, cf admit <id> <key> by existing member admits
- Membership Merkle hash is updated on join/leave
- Unit tests for join/leave/admit, membership hash computation
close_reason: Implemented cf join + cf leave + cf admit + cf members. Added pkg/message (Message, ProvenanceHop, signing/verification). Extended pkg/transport/fs with RemoveMember, WriteMessage, ListMessages. System messages (campfire:member-joined, campfire:member-left) written on join/leave with signed provenance hops. Open campfires allow immediate join; invite-only requires cf admit. All tests pass, two-agent E2E verified for both join protocols.

title: cf send + cf read + cf inspect: members exchange signed messages with provenance chains
description:
Members can send messages to a campfire and other members can read them. Every message has a valid signature and provenance chain. cf inspect shows the full provenance chain for any message.

## Implementation
- pkg/message/: Message struct (id, sender, payload, tags, timestamp, signature, provenance), construction, signing, verification
- pkg/message/: ProvenanceHop construction — campfire signs the hop with membership hash, member count, join protocol, reception requirements
- pkg/transport/fs/: write messages to shared directory, read messages from it
- pkg/store/: store received messages in local SQLite
- pkg/campfire/: broadcast logic — verify sender sig, apply filter_out, append provenance hop, deliver to members via filter_in
- cmd/cf/: send, read, inspect subcommands

## Filter behavior (Phase 1)
- Manual only: pass_through list and suppress list on each member edge
- No optimization loop
- Reception requirements automatically in pass_through

## Done condition
- cf send <campfire-id> "hello" --tag status-update sends a signed message
- cf read <campfire-id> shows messages with sender, timestamp, tags, payload
- cf read --json outputs structured JSON array
- cf read (no campfire-id) shows messages across all campfires
- cf inspect <message-id> shows the full provenance chain with each hop's campfire ID, membership hash, member count, timestamp, and signature verification status
- Messages from non-members are rejected
- Invalid signatures are rejected
- Unit tests for signing, verification, provenance chain construction and verification
close_reason: Implemented cf send + cf read + cf inspect. cf send creates signed messages with provenance hops written to transport dir. cf read syncs from filesystem into local SQLite, displays unread messages, manages read cursors (--all, --peek flags). cf inspect shows full provenance chain with signature verification status for message and each hop. All signatures verify. Message package has full test coverage. Read cursor behavior verified: second read shows 'No new messages', new messages after cursor update appear correctly.

title: cf discover: agents find campfires through filesystem beacon directory
description:
Agents can discover campfires by scanning the beacon directory. This is the bootstrap mechanism — how an agent finds campfires to join without out-of-band communication.

## Implementation
- pkg/beacon/: scan ~/.campfire/beacons/, parse beacon files, verify signatures
- cmd/cf/: discover subcommand

## Done condition
- cf discover lists all beacons in ~/.campfire/beacons/ with campfire ID, join protocol, description, reception requirements, transport info
- cf discover --json outputs structured JSON
- Invalid/corrupted beacon files are skipped with a warning
- Beacon signature verification: beacons with invalid signatures are flagged
- Unit tests for beacon parsing and signature verification
close_reason: Implemented cf discover. Scans beacon directory, displays campfire ID, join protocol, description, signature verification status, transport, and reception requirements. Both human and --json output. Empty directory handled gracefully.

title: cf dm: private conversation sugar works end-to-end
description:
cf dm is sugar that automates: create a two-member campfire (or reuse existing), send invitation through a shared campfire, deliver the message. This is the convenience command for quick agent-to-agent communication.

## Implementation
- cmd/cf/: dm subcommand
- Logic: check if a 2-member campfire already exists with target, if so reuse it, otherwise create one and send invite through a shared campfire

## Done condition
- cf dm <target-public-key> "message" delivers a message to the target agent
- If no existing DM campfire exists, one is created automatically
- If a DM campfire already exists, it is reused
- The target agent can cf read the DM campfire and see the message
- cf dm --json outputs the sent message as structured JSON
- Unit tests for DM campfire reuse logic
close_reason: Implemented cf dm. Creates invite-only 2-member campfire on first DM, reuses on subsequent DMs. Pre-admits target by writing member record at creation time. cf join updated to detect pre-admitted members and register locally without re-adding to transport. Full E2E verified: A creates DM, B discovers beacon, B joins, B reads messages, B replies, A reads reply.

title: Dogfood: two Claude Code sessions coordinate through a campfire
description:
Integration test proving the full system works end-to-end. Two separate agent identities on one machine go through the complete workflow.

## Scenario
1. Agent A: cf init (gets identity A)
2. Agent B: cf init (gets identity B) — in a different home dir or with --config flag
3. Agent A: cf create --protocol open --transport fs --beacon fs --description "coordination"
4. Agent A: cf ls — sees the campfire
5. Agent B: cf discover — sees the beacon
6. Agent B: cf join <campfire-id>
7. Agent A: cf read — sees member-joined
8. Agent A: cf members <campfire-id> — sees both agents
9. Agent A: cf send <campfire-id> "starting task X" --tag status-update
10. Agent B: cf read <campfire-id> — sees the message
11. Agent B: cf inspect <message-id> — verifies provenance chain (1 hop, valid signatures)
12. Agent B: cf send <campfire-id> "task X blocked on Y" --tag status-update
13. Agent A: cf read — sees both messages
14. Agent A: cf dm <agent-b-key> "can you unblock Y?"
15. Agent B: cf read — sees DM message in a separate campfire
16. Agent B: cf leave <campfire-id>
17. Agent A: cf read — sees member-left
18. Agent A: cf disband <campfire-id>

## Done condition
- All 18 steps complete successfully in a scripted test
- All signatures verify
- All provenance chains are valid
- --json output is parseable at every step
- The test runs in CI (containerized)
- No manual intervention required
close_reason: Full 18-step integration test passes. Two agents: init identity, create campfire, discover beacon, join, exchange signed messages, inspect provenance chains (all signatures valid), send DMs (create + reuse), leave, disband. Scripted test at tests/integration_test.sh. Fixed DM reuse bug — now only reuses invite-only 2-member campfires, not open campfires that happen to have 2 members.

title: GitHub transport: comment encode/decode (campfire-msg-v1 format)

title: GitHub transport: REST API client (thin wrapper over GitHub comments API)
description:
OUTCOME: pkg/transport/github/client.go exists with a githubClient struct that can create, list, and conditionally GET issue comments. All client tests pass against a fake HTTP server.

CONTEXT:
- Design doc: docs/transport-github-design.md §4 (Delivery Model), §5 (Rate Limits), §9 (TransportConfig Schema)
- Required API calls:
  - POST /repos/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/comments  → create comment
  - GET  /repos/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/comments?since={ISO8601}&per_page=100 → list comments
  - GET with If-None-Match / ETag headers for conditional requests (304 = no new comments, does not consume rate limit quota)
  - POST /repos/{repo}/issues  → create issue (for campfire creation)
  - GET  /repos/{repo}/contents/.campfire/beacons/{id}.json → read beacon file
  - PUT  /repos/{repo}/contents/.campfire/beacons/{id}.json → write beacon file (for PublishBeacon)
- Config.BaseURL defaults to "https://api.github.com"; configurable for GitHub Enterprise
- Token passed as Authorization: Bearer {token} header
- Rate limit: write queue with minimum 750ms between comment creates on same issue (secondary rate limit mitigation per design doc §5)
- 30s default poll interval (but client itself is stateless — poll loop is in transport.go)

STRUCT:
  type githubClient struct {
    baseURL    string
    token      string
    httpClient *http.Client
    writeMu    sync.Mutex
    lastWrite  map[string]time.Time // issue key -> last write time
  }

CONSTRAINTS:
- No polling loop here — that is in transport.go (separate bead)
- The 750ms write throttle lives in the client (it is per-issue, per-client)
- No retry logic in v1 — return errors, let callers handle
- Use net/http stdlib only — no external HTTP client libraries

DONE CONDITION:
- pkg/transport/github/client.go: githubClient struct with CreateComment, ListComments(since time.Time, etag string) returns (comments, newEtag, error), CreateIssue, GetFile, PutFile methods
- pkg/transport/github/client_test.go: tests against httptest.NewServer fake — verifies Authorization header, since parameter, If-None-Match / ETag handling, 304 response returns empty list, write throttle (two rapid CreateComment calls to same issue: second call delayed >= 750ms)
- go test ./pkg/transport/github/... passes

title: PollBroker: in-process fan-out for long-poll goroutines
description:
OUTCOME: pkg/transport/http/poll_broker.go exists, passes its own unit tests, and is embedded in Transport with zero behavior change to existing tests.

DONE CONDITION: go test ./pkg/transport/http/... passes with PollBroker wired into Transport.New(). The broker is initialized, embedded, and callable — but handleDeliver does not yet call Notify (that is workspace-15). Existing http_test.go tests remain green.

CONTEXT (read before touching code):
- Design doc: docs/transport-longpoll-design.md §"PollBroker" and §"Summary of Changes"
- Existing transport: pkg/transport/http/transport.go — Transport struct, New() function
- PollBroker is an unexported-from-outside-the-package type (the http package is internal to campfire; it is fine to use an exported name PollBroker within the package).

WHAT TO BUILD:
File: pkg/transport/http/poll_broker.go

PollBroker struct (all fields unexported):
  mu             sync.Mutex
  subs           map[string][]chan struct{}  // campfireID → notification channels
  limits         map[string]int             // campfireID → active poller count
  maxPerCampfire int                        // default 64

Methods:
  Subscribe(campfireID string) (<-chan struct{}, func(), error)
    - Under lock: check limits[campfireID] < maxPerCampfire, else return nil, nil, error
    - Allocate make(chan struct{}, 1) — buffered capacity-1 so Notify is never blocking under lock
    - Append to subs[campfireID], increment limits[campfireID]
    - Return channel, deregister closure (removes channel from subs, decrements limits), nil

  Notify(campfireID string)
    - Under lock: for each ch in subs[campfireID]: select { case ch <- struct{}{}: default: }
    - Non-blocking send: pollers that are still in the sync-then-block handoff miss the signal
      but catch up via the initial ListMessages query on reconnect (design §"Notify semantics")

  NotifyEviction(campfireID, memberPubKey string)
    - No-op stub. Signature must exist now so handleMembership can call it without a file change later.
    - Design marks forced eviction as a "nice-to-have". Correctness holds without it (403 on reconnect).

Transport changes (transport.go):
  - Add field: pollBroker *PollBroker
  - Initialize in New(): t.pollBroker = &PollBroker{subs: make(map[string][]chan struct{}), limits: make(map[string]int), maxPerCampfire: 64}

TESTS TO WRITE FIRST (poll_broker_test.go):
  TestPollBrokerSubscribeNotify: subscribe two channels to same campfire, call Notify, assert both receive signal within 10ms.
  TestPollBrokerDeregister: subscribe, call deregister fn, call Notify, assert channel receives nothing within 10ms.
  TestPollBrokerLimit: subscribe maxPerCampfire times, assert next Subscribe returns non-nil error. After one deregister, assert Subscribe succeeds.
  TestPollBrokerMultiCampfire: subscribe to campfireA and campfireB, Notify(campfireA), assert only campfireA channel fires within 10ms and campfireB does not.

CONSTRAINTS:
  - No goroutines inside the broker. Goroutines live in handlePoll (workspace-15).
  - Buffered channel capacity 1 is sufficient: at most one notification is pending per poller at any time.
  - The deregister closure must be safe to call more than once (idempotent or guarded with a sync.Once).

title: GitHub transport: beacon publish and discovery (.campfire/beacons/ in coordination repo)
description:
OUTCOME: pkg/transport/github/beacon.go exists. PublishBeacon commits a beacon JSON file to the coordination repo. DiscoverBeacons lists and verifies beacon files from the repo. All tests pass.

CONTEXT:
- Design doc: docs/transport-github-design.md §3 (Discovery)
- Beacon file path: .campfire/beacons/{campfire-id}.json
- Beacon JSON structure (from design doc §3):
  {
    "campfire_id": "<hex-ed25519-pubkey>",
    "join_protocol": "open",
    "reception_requirements": [],
    "transport": {
      "protocol": "github",
      "config": { "repo": "org/campfire-relay", "issue_number": 42, "issue_url": "..." }
    },
    "description": "...",
    "signature": "<hex-ed25519-sig>"
  }
- Uses githubClient.GetFile (list beacons) and githubClient.PutFile (publish)
- Signature verification: each beacon signature must be verified against its campfire_id (Ed25519 pubkey)
- pkg/identity package handles Ed25519 sign/verify — use it, do not re-implement crypto

CONSTRAINTS:
- DiscoverBeacons silently skips beacons with invalid signatures (log and continue, do not fail entire discovery)
- PublishBeacon requires Contents: Read+Write token scope — document in function comment
- No changes to existing beacon package or protocol spec — this is transport-specific beacon I/O only

DONE CONDITION:
- pkg/transport/github/beacon.go: Beacon struct, PublishBeacon(client, campfireID, beacon Beacon), DiscoverBeacons(client) returns []Beacon
- pkg/transport/github/beacon_test.go: tests against httptest.NewServer fake — verifies beacon JSON encoding, signature verification rejects tampered beacon, missing campfire_id returns error, valid beacon is returned in discovery list
- go test ./pkg/transport/github/... passes
notes: Pass 2 TDD gap: add test for DiscoverBeacons when .campfire/beacons/ directory is empty — must return ([]Beacon{}, nil), not an error.

title: GitHub transport: Transport struct, poll loop, Send/Poll lifecycle
description:
OUTCOME: pkg/transport/github/transport.go exists. The Transport struct starts a poll loop, sends messages via CreateComment, and polls for new messages with conditional GETs. Tests cover the full send/poll cycle against a fake GitHub server.

CONTEXT:
- Design doc: docs/transport-github-design.md §8 (Transport Struct, Poll Loop), §4 (Delivery Model)
- Transport struct per design doc §8:
    type Config struct {
      Repo string; IssueNumber int; Token string
      PollIntervalSecs int   // default 30
      EncryptAtRest    bool
      BaseURL          string // default "https://api.github.com"
    }
    type Transport struct {
      cfg Config; client *githubClient; store *store.Store
      mu sync.RWMutex
      lastSeen map[string]time.Time  // campfireID -> last comment timestamp
      etagCache map[string]string    // campfireID -> last ETag
      stopCh chan struct{}
    }
- Start() spawns pollLoop goroutine per registered campfire; Stop() closes stopCh
- Send(campfireID, msg): calls EncodeComment → githubClient.CreateComment; stores in local SQLite via store
- Poll(campfireID): calls githubClient.ListComments(since, etag); for each comment: DecodeComment → verify Ed25519 signature → store via store; updates lastSeen and etagCache
- RegisterCampfire(campfireID string, issueNumber int): registers a campfire so the poll loop tracks it
- CreateCampfire(c *campfire.Campfire, description string) (int, error): calls githubClient.CreateIssue; returns issue number
- pollLoop per design doc §8 code block — ticker-based, select on stopCh
- DEPENDENCIES: workspace-10 (EncodeComment/DecodeComment), workspace-11 (githubClient)

CONSTRAINTS:
- store package: use pkg/store (same as http transport does)
- Ed25519 verification: use pkg/identity — do not skip verification even in tests; use test keypairs
- No webhook receiver in v1 (design doc §11 open question #3 — explicitly deferred)
- EncryptAtRest=true path: AES-256-GCM with HKDF-derived key from campfire Ed25519 key material (design doc §6); only implement if you have time within this bead — if not, document as a follow-up bead. YAGNI check: if encrypt_at_rest=false is the default and unencrypted is sufficient for v1, skip the encrypt path and create a blocker bead.

DONE CONDITION:
- pkg/transport/github/transport.go: Transport, Config structs; New, Start, Stop, Send, Poll, RegisterCampfire, CreateCampfire methods
- pkg/transport/github/transport_test.go: tests against httptest.NewServer — verifies Send posts correct comment body, Poll decodes and verifies message, 304 response advances no state, Start/Stop lifecycle (goroutine exits cleanly), duplicate message not stored twice
- go test ./pkg/transport/github/... passes
notes: Pass 2 TDD: EncryptAtRest is explicitly YAGNI-deferred for v1. Do NOT implement AES-256-GCM path. Add a test asserting Config{EncryptAtRest: true} returns an ErrEncryptAtRestNotSupported error from New() (so callers fail fast rather than silently sending plaintext when they expected encryption). Create workspace-17 as a blocked follow-up bead if the encrypt path is needed.

title: handlePoll: GET /campfire/{id}/poll server endpoint with fan-out wiring
description:
OUTCOME: GET /campfire/{id}/poll is registered in handler.go route(), handlePoll() implements sync-then-block semantics, and handleDeliver calls pollBroker.Notify() after every successful store write. Integration test with two goroutines passes.

DONE CONDITION: go test ./pkg/transport/http/... passes including new poll endpoint tests. A test can POST a message to /deliver and observe it arrive on a concurrent GET /poll within 100ms.

CONTEXT (read before touching code):
- Design doc: docs/transport-longpoll-design.md — §"Endpoint Specification", §"PollBroker", §"Interaction with Existing Endpoints", §"Failure Modes"
- Existing route dispatch: pkg/transport/http/handler.go route() switch statement (add case before default)
- Existing handleSync: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleSync() — poll reuses same store query and auth pattern. Study it before implementing handlePoll.
- PollBroker (built in workspace-12): pkg/transport/http/poll_broker.go
- Store query: store.ListMessages(campfireID, since) returns []MessageRecord ordered by ReceivedAt ascending
- Membership check: store.GetMembership() returns the LOCAL agent membership record only (not the member list). The member list is in peer_endpoints table. Use store.ListPeerEndpoints(campfireID) to get []PeerEndpoint and check senderHex against MemberPubkey. Also check h.transport.SelfInfo() pubKeyHex — the local agent may not appear in peer_endpoints.
- Null-broker guard: tests that construct handler{} directly without Transport will have h.transport == nil. If h.transport == nil or h.transport.pollBroker == nil, respond 501 Not Implemented.

WHAT TO BUILD:
1. handler.go route(): add before default: case action == "poll" && r.Method == http.MethodGet: h.handlePoll(w, r, campfireID)

2. handler.go handlePoll(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request, campfireID string):
   a. Null guard: if h.transport == nil || h.transport.pollBroker == nil { http.Error(501) ; return }
   b. Verify auth: verifyRequestSignature(senderHex, sigB64, []byte{}) — 401 on failure (same as handleSync)
   c. Membership check: ListPeerEndpoints + selfPubKeyHex, 403 if senderHex not found
   d. Parse query params: since (int64, default 0), timeout (int, default 30, cap at 120). 400 on parse error.
   e. Subscribe: h.transport.pollBroker.Subscribe(campfireID) — 503 if limit exceeded
   f. Defer deregister()
   g. Initial sync: store.ListMessages(campfireID, since). If len > 0: respond 200 with CBOR body and X-Campfire-Cursor = strconv.FormatInt(records[len-1].ReceivedAt, 10). Return.
   h. Block: select { case <-ch: case <-time.After(time.Duration(timeout) * time.Second): }
   i. Post-wait query: store.ListMessages(campfireID, since). If len > 0: respond 200. Else: respond 204 with X-Campfire-Cursor: strconv.FormatInt(since, 10).

3. handler.go handleDeliver(): immediately after h.store.AddMessage(rec) succeeds, add:
   if h.transport != nil && h.transport.pollBroker != nil { h.transport.pollBroker.Notify(campfireID) }

RESPONSE FORMAT:
  200: Content-Type: application/cbor, X-Campfire-Cursor: <decimal nanosecond int64>, body: CBOR []message.Message
  204: no body, X-Campfire-Cursor: <decimal since value>
  401/403/400/503/501: http.Error() plain text

NOTE on cursor: X-Campfire-Cursor header must be set BEFORE calling w.WriteHeader() since headers cannot be set after the response is started in Go net/http.

TESTS TO WRITE FIRST (add to http_test.go or new poll_handler_test.go):
  TestHandlePollImmediateMessages: store 2 messages before poll, assert 200 with both, cursor = ReceivedAt of newest.
  TestHandlePollTimeout: poll with timeout=1, no messages delivered, assert 204 within 2s, cursor = since.
  TestHandlePollWakeOnDeliver: goroutine blocks on poll (timeout=30), concurrent goroutine POSTs /deliver, assert poll 200 within 200ms.
  TestHandlePollUnauthorized: missing headers, assert 401.
  TestHandlePollNonMember: valid sig but sender not in peer list, assert 403.
  TestHandlePollInvalidParams: since="abc", assert 400.
  TestHandlePollLimitExceeded: exhaust broker limit, next poll returns 503.

CONSTRAINTS:
  - Do not change handleSync or other handlers beyond the one Notify() call in handleDeliver.
  - workspace-12 (PollBroker) must be complete before this bead is started.

title: Poll(): client function for long-poll in peer.go
description:
OUTCOME: Poll() function exists in pkg/transport/http/peer.go and is callable by the cf read command. Unit tests against a test server pass.

DONE CONDITION: go test ./pkg/transport/http/... passes including Poll() tests. Function signature matches the design doc exactly. A test server running handlePoll can be called and returns messages correctly.

CONTEXT (read before touching code):
- Design doc: docs/transport-longpoll-design.md §"Client API" and §"Reconnection and Gap Recovery"
- Existing client functions for pattern reference: Sync(), Deliver() in pkg/transport/http/peer.go
- signRequest() is already in peer.go — reuse it for auth headers
- httpClient is already declared (var httpClient = &http.Client{Timeout: 30 * time.Second}) — but Poll needs a longer timeout than 30s since the server holds for up to 120s. Use a per-request context or a dedicated client with timeout = timeoutSecs + 5s.

WHAT TO BUILD:
Add to pkg/transport/http/peer.go:

func Poll(endpoint, campfireID string, cursor int64, timeoutSecs int, id *identity.Identity) ([]message.Message, int64, error)

Implementation:
  1. Build URL: fmt.Sprintf("%s/campfire/%s/poll?since=%d&timeout=%d", endpoint, campfireID, cursor, timeoutSecs)
  2. Create GET request with no body.
  3. Sign with empty body: signRequest(req, id, []byte{})
  4. Use a timeout-aware http.Client: &http.Client{Timeout: time.Duration(timeoutSecs+5) * time.Second}
     (5s buffer over the server-side timeout so the OS does not cut the connection before the server responds)
  5. Execute request.
  6. On 204: read X-Campfire-Cursor header (should equal cursor). Return (nil, cursor, nil).
  7. On 200: read X-Campfire-Cursor header → parse as int64 → newCursor. Read body, CBOR-decode []message.Message. Return (msgs, newCursor, nil).
  8. On any other status: return error with status code and body text.

Return semantics (from design doc):
  - (nil, cursor, nil)   → server timed out, reconnect with same cursor
  - (msgs, newCursor, nil) → messages received, reconnect with newCursor
  - (nil, cursor, err)  → transport error, back off and retry

TESTS TO WRITE FIRST (poll_client_test.go, or add to existing http_test.go pattern):
  TestPollReceivesMessages: start httptest.NewServer with handlePoll, pre-store 2 messages, call Poll(cursor=0), assert msgs returned and newCursor = ReceivedAt of newest.
  TestPollTimeout: start httptest.NewServer, no messages, call Poll(timeout=1), assert returns (nil, cursor, nil) within 2s.
  TestPollAuthFailure: server returns 401, assert Poll returns non-nil error.
  TestPollCursorAdvances: call Poll twice, second call passes cursor from first, assert no duplicates.

CONSTRAINTS:
  - Do not modify httpClient (shared package var); use a local client with computed timeout.
  - Function must match the signature in the design doc exactly — cf read will call it by name.
  - workspace-15 (handlePoll) must be complete before integration tests can run end-to-end, but unit tests using httptest.Server can be written against the real handler.
  - No reconnect loop inside Poll(). The caller (cf read) owns the loop.

title: cf read: polling mode for NAT'd agents (no inbound listener)
description:
OUTCOME: `cf read` works for agents with no inbound listener (selfEndpoint == ""). When selfEndpoint is empty, the command polls a known peer instead of reading from local store. The --follow flag keeps the process alive and streams messages to stdout as they arrive.

DONE CONDITION: A NAT'd agent can run `cf read <campfire-id>` and receive messages sent by other peers. Without --follow, it drains available messages and exits. With --follow, it blocks and prints messages as they arrive until Ctrl-C. Existing `cf read` behavior (direct mode, selfEndpoint != "") is unchanged.

CONTEXT (read before touching code):
- Design doc: docs/transport-longpoll-design.md §"cf read" and §"Peer Unavailability"
- Existing cf read command: cmd/cf/ — find the read subcommand. Read the full file before editing.
- Poll() client function: pkg/transport/http/peer.go — func Poll(endpoint, campfireID string, cursor int64, timeoutSecs int, id *identity.Identity) ([]message.Message, int64, error) (workspace-17, must be complete first)
- selfEndpoint is the --endpoint flag value (or empty string if not set). This is how NAT mode is detected.
- Peer list: store.ListPeerEndpoints(campfireID) returns []store.PeerEndpoint. Each has .Endpoint string. Filter to non-empty endpoints as poll targets.
- Initial cursor: call store.ListMessages(campfireID, 0) and take the max ReceivedAt, or 0 if empty.

WHAT TO BUILD:
Modify the cf read command:

1. After loading identity and campfire state, detect NAT mode: selfEndpoint == "".
2. If selfEndpoint != "": existing behavior unchanged (read from local store, print, exit).
3. If selfEndpoint == "" (NAT mode):
   a. peers := store.ListPeerEndpoints(campfireID); filter to non-empty Endpoint.
   b. If len(peers) == 0: fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, "no reachable peers to poll"); os.Exit(1)
   c. cursor := computeInitialCursor(store, campfireID)  // max ReceivedAt or 0
   d. peerIdx := 0
   e. Loop:
      msgs, newCursor, err := Poll(peers[peerIdx].Endpoint, campfireID, cursor, 30, id)
      if err != nil:
        peerIdx = (peerIdx + 1) % len(peers)
        time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
        continue
      if len(msgs) > 0:
        printMessages(msgs)  // same format as existing read
        cursor = newCursor
      if !follow: break
   f. If follow: handle os.Signal (SIGINT/SIGTERM) for clean exit.

4. Add --follow flag (bool, default false) to the read cobra command.

TESTS TO WRITE FIRST:
  TestCfReadNATModeReceivesMessages: construct a real httptest.Server running handlePoll with a pre-stored message. Call the read command logic (not os/exec) with selfEndpoint="" and one peer pointing to the test server. Assert the message is printed to stdout and the command exits (not --follow mode).
  TestCfReadNATModeNoPeers: selfEndpoint="", no peers in store. Assert command exits with error and prints the expected error message.
  TestCfReadDirectModeUnchanged: selfEndpoint="http://localhost:9999" (non-empty). Assert command reads from local store as before (no Poll() call).
  TestCfReadFollowTerminatesOnSignal: selfEndpoint="", --follow=true. After receiving one message, send SIGINT to the goroutine running the command, assert it exits cleanly.

CONSTRAINTS:
  - Do not break existing cf read behavior (direct mode, selfEndpoint != "").
  - workspace-17 (Poll() function) must be complete before this bead is started.
  - No new configuration files or store schema changes. Peer list comes from existing ListPeerEndpoints.
  - The --follow flag uses the tail -f convention (long-lived process, stream output).
  - Cursor persistence: persist cursor in memory only within the session. On restart, derive cursor from max ReceivedAt in local store (or 0). Do NOT add a new store table — that is a future enhancement.

title: GitHub transport: Ed25519-to-X25519 key conversion for encrypted key delivery on join
description:
OUTCOME: pkg/identity/x25519.go exists with Ed25519ToX25519 conversion. Encrypted key delivery (encrypt-to-pubkey, decrypt-with-privkey) works correctly. All tests pass.

CONTEXT:
- Required by: workspace-16 (CLI join flow) — key material delivery on GitHub transport uses encrypted comments
- Design doc: docs/transport-github-design.md §7 (cf join), §6 (Security Model)
- On join (threshold=1): admitting member encrypts campfire private key bytes to joiner Ed25519 pubkey converted to X25519 via RFC 7748; joiner decrypts using their Ed25519 private key converted to X25519
- RFC 7748 Ed25519→X25519 conversion: well-specified, deterministic; the public key conversion clamps bits per spec
- Encryption scheme: X25519 ECDH + HKDF-SHA256 for key derivation + AES-256-GCM for payload encryption
  - Ephemeral X25519 keypair per encryption operation (sender generates it fresh)
  - ECDH shared secret = X25519(ephemeral_priv, recipient_x25519_pub)
  - Key material = HKDF-SHA256(shared_secret, info="campfire-key-delivery", salt=nonce)
  - Ciphertext = nonce || AES-256-GCM(key_material, plaintext)
  - Sender includes ephemeral_x25519_pubkey in the encrypted blob so recipient can perform ECDH

CONSTRAINTS:
- Go stdlib only: crypto/ecdh (for X25519), golang.org/x/crypto/hkdf, crypto/aes, crypto/cipher, crypto/rand
- The conversion from Ed25519 to X25519 uses the Curve25519 cofactor clamping: see filippo.io/edwards25519 for reference, but do NOT add that as a dependency — implement directly using the field arithmetic described in RFC 7748 §4.1 (or use the stdlib crypto/ecdh package which handles this)
- Do NOT add this to pkg/transport/github/ — it belongs in pkg/identity/ since it is identity-level crypto
- Do NOT change existing identity.go — add new file x25519.go

DONE CONDITION:
- pkg/identity/x25519.go: Ed25519ToX25519Pub(ed25519PubKey []byte) ([]byte, error), Ed25519ToX25519Priv(ed25519PrivKey []byte) ([]byte, error), EncryptToEd25519Key(recipientEd25519Pub []byte, plaintext []byte) ([]byte, error), DecryptWithEd25519Key(recipientEd25519Priv []byte, ciphertext []byte) ([]byte, error)
- pkg/identity/x25519_test.go: tests — round-trip encrypt/decrypt, wrong private key fails decrypt, tampered ciphertext fails AEAD verification, Ed25519→X25519 pub conversion matches known test vectors (RFC 7748 §6.1 or test vector from wycheproof or similar)
- go test ./pkg/identity/... passes

title: Spec: add antecedents to Message, define future/fulfills pattern
description:
Update docs/protocol-spec.md:

1. Add `antecedents: [uuid]` to the Message struct. Always present, empty array when no references.
2. Add antecedents to the signature coverage: sender signs (id + payload + tags + antecedents + timestamp).
3. Add a new "Message DAG" section in Primitives explaining the antecedents field and what the DAG represents (causal/semantic relationships, orthogonal to provenance which tracks routing).
4. Add a "Futures and Fulfillment" subsection in Operations defining the pattern:
   - A future is a message tagged `future`. Its payload describes what qualifies as fulfillment.
   - A fulfillment is a message tagged `fulfills` with the future as an antecedent.
   - A dependent message references a future as an antecedent — agents treat it as actionable when the future is fulfilled.
   - Show the full coordination example: declare future, chain dependent work, fulfillment unblocks the chain.
5. Note that fulfillment validation and activation semantics are agent-local, not protocol-enforced.
6. Update Security Considerations: antecedents are claims, not proofs — a message can reference an ID the recipient has never seen (cross-campfire, or simply not yet received). This does not weaken identity or enable spoofing — the antecedent field is covered by the sender signature and cannot be tampered with.
7. Update Recursive Composition section: when a child campfire relays a message to a parent, antecedents travel with it. The parent may not have the referenced messages (they live in the child). This is fine — antecedents are informational. The child campfire opacity is preserved; the parent cannot resolve antecedents into the child.
8. Update phase1-design.md: add Antecedents to MessageSignInput CBOR struct (new field 5, bump timestamp to 6), add antecedents TEXT column (JSON array of UUIDs) to messages table in SQLite schema.

title: Integration test: future/fulfillment coordination pattern
description:
Scripted integration test demonstrating the full future/fulfillment coordination pattern with two agents.

## Scenario
1. Agent A sends a future: "need schema review" (--future --tag schema-review)
2. Agent A sends dependent work: "run migration after review" --antecedent <future-id>
3. Agent A sends another dependent: "deploy after migration" --antecedent <migration-msg-id> (transitive chain — depends on step 2, not directly on the future)
4. Agent B reads, sees the future (tagged "future", no fulfillment yet)
5. Agent B fulfills: "schema approved" --fulfills <future-id>
6. Both agents read. Verify via --json:
   - The future message has a fulfillment (referenced_by includes the fulfills message)
   - The migration message antecedent points to the future
   - The deploy message antecedent points to the migration message
   - The DAG structure is: future ← fulfillment, future ← migration ← deploy
7. Agent A inspects the future — sees fulfillment and dependents in referenced_by
8. Verify all signatures valid, all provenance chains valid, all antecedent references correct in --json

NOTE: The protocol does not enforce activation semantics. The test verifies DAG structure, not "unblocked" state. Whether agents treat messages as actionable based on antecedent fulfillment is agent-local behavior.

- All steps pass
- --json output at each step is parseable with correct antecedents/referenced_by
- tests/integration_test.sh still passes (existing behavior unchanged)

title: Spec: threshold signatures, eviction rekey, P2P transport model
description:
Update docs/protocol-spec.md with threshold signature model and P2P transport:

## Campfire struct changes
- Add threshold field to Campfire struct (uint, minimum signers for provenance hops)
- threshold=1 means any single member can sign (shared key, equivalent to current filesystem model)
- threshold=N means all members must cooperate
- Default: threshold=1 for backward compatibility

## Provenance hop signing
- "Signed by the campfire" means "signed via threshold scheme by M-of-N members"
- Verification is identical regardless of threshold (same Ed25519 public key, same verify)
- The signing ceremony (single signer vs multi-round threshold) is a transport/implementation concern
- Spec defines the property: a valid provenance hop signature proves that at least M members cooperated

## Eviction
- Eviction triggers key re-sharing excluding the evicted member
- For threshold > 1: FROST-style re-sharing preserves the campfire public key. No identity change. No beacon updates. No parent campfire notification needed.
- For threshold = 1 (shared key): evicted member has the full key, so re-sharing cannot preserve identity. Must generate new keypair. campfire:rekey message links old identity to new, signed by old key. Beacons update. Parent campfires accept the transition.
- Define campfire:rekey system message: {old_key, new_key, reason}, signed by old key

## P2P transport model
- Add section on P2P HTTP transport as the primary internet transport
- No relay: members communicate directly with each other
- Each member runs a handler (deliver, sync, membership endpoints)
- Send = fan-out to all members. Gossip for reliability.
- Catch-up via sync endpoint (polling)
- NAT: polling mode for members behind NAT
- Campfire survives as long as any member is alive
- Beacon includes member endpoint(s), not a relay URL

## Transport spectrum
- filesystem: shared directory, threshold=1 implicit
- p2p-http: direct member-to-member, threshold configurable
- (future) chain: on-chain membership, threshold via smart contract

## Done condition
- Protocol spec updated with threshold field, rekey semantics, P2P transport model
- Existing filesystem transport section still valid (threshold=1 case)
- Security Considerations updated for threshold model and split prevention
- Recursive Composition updated: rekey affects child-in-parent membership for threshold=1 only
close_reason: Updated protocol spec with: threshold field on Campfire struct, Threshold Signatures section (threshold=1 shared key vs threshold>1 FROST-style, split prevention properties), Eviction and Rekey section (threshold>1 preserves identity, threshold=1 rekeys with campfire:rekey succession message), P2P HTTP transport model (no relay, member-to-member direct, gossip for reliability, polling for NAT, join flow with key material distribution), updated Security Considerations (malicious campfire per threshold, threshold security, split prevention, antecedent references).

title: pkg/threshold: FROST wrapper — DKG, signing rounds, re-sharing
description:
Wrap taurushq-io/frost-ed25519 in a campfire-specific package that handles distributed key generation, threshold signing, and key re-sharing.

## What to build
- pkg/threshold/threshold.go: high-level API wrapping frost-ed25519
- KeyGen(participants []ParticipantID, threshold int) — runs DKG, returns each participant share + group public key
- Sign(message []byte, myShare, participants) — initiates/participates in a signing round, returns Ed25519 signature
- ReShare(currentParticipants, newParticipants, threshold) — redistributes shares preserving public key (for join/evict)
- The API must handle the round-based protocol: produce outgoing messages, accept incoming messages, output result

## Key constraint
- Signatures produced by Sign() MUST verify with standard ed25519.Verify(groupPublicKey, message, signature)
- This is guaranteed by the frost-ed25519 library but must be tested explicitly

## The round-based challenge
- FROST signing is multi-round: each signer produces a commitment (round 1), then a signature share (round 2)
- The API needs to handle this as message exchange, not function calls — signers are on different machines
- Design: SignRound1() -> outgoing commitment, ProcessCommitments(incoming) -> SignRound2() -> outgoing share, ProcessShares(incoming) -> final signature

## Done condition
- DKG produces shares for N participants with threshold M, group public key is standard Ed25519
- Sign with M shares produces signature that verifies with ed25519.Verify()
- Sign with M-1 shares fails
- ReShare excludes a participant, remaining participants get new shares, public key unchanged
- Unit tests for all of the above
- go test ./pkg/threshold/... passes
close_reason: Restructuring: FROST re-sharing not available in Go libraries. Redesigning bead tree to ship FROST with DKG+signing, defer re-sharing. Eviction always rekeys for now.

title: Integration tests: P2P HTTP, threshold signing, eviction

title: Add threshold field to campfire structs, store, and CLI
description:
Add the threshold parameter throughout the codebase. This is plumbing — no FROST logic, no HTTP transport. Just the field.

## Why threshold
See docs/protocol-spec.md "Threshold Signatures" section. The threshold field determines how many members must cooperate to sign provenance hops. threshold=1 means any single member (current behavior). threshold>1 requires FROST multi-party signing.

## What to change

### pkg/campfire/campfire.go
- Add Threshold uint field to Campfire struct (after ReceptionRequirements)
- Add Threshold uint field to CampfireState struct
- campfire.New() accepts threshold parameter
- Default threshold=1

### pkg/store/store.go
- Add threshold INTEGER column to campfire_memberships table (default 1)
- Add Threshold field to Membership struct
- Update AddMembership, GetMembership, ListMemberships queries

### cmd/cf/cmd/create.go
- Add --threshold flag (uint, default 1)
- Pass to campfire.New()
- Include in CampfireState written to transport directory
- Include in SQLite membership record

### cmd/cf/cmd/ls.go
- Show threshold in output (both human and JSON)

### Filesystem transport
- Threshold is stored in campfire.cbor but always 1 for filesystem transport
- No behavioral change — filesystem transport cannot do threshold > 1

### docs/phase1-design.md
- Add threshold field to CampfireState CBOR struct
- Add threshold column to SQLite schema
- Note: filesystem transport always threshold=1

## Done condition
- cf create --threshold 2 stores threshold=2 in campfire.cbor and SQLite
- cf create (no flag) defaults to threshold=1
- cf ls shows threshold
- cf ls --json includes threshold field
- Existing tests pass unchanged (all use default threshold=1)
- go test ./... passes
close_reason: Threshold field added to Campfire/CampfireState structs (cbor key 6), store schema (threshold INTEGER column), create command (--threshold flag), and ls output. All existing tests pass.

title: P2P HTTP campfire lifecycle: create, join with key exchange
description:
Create and join campfires over P2P HTTP transport with key material exchange. This wires the HTTP transport (workspace-4.7) into the campfire lifecycle.

## Design decisions (from protocol design session)
- **P2P not relay**: the campfire has no server. Members are the infrastructure.
- **Key distribution**: on join, the admitting member sends the campfire private key (threshold=1) or initiates DKG (threshold>1), encrypted to the joiner public key.
- **Join triggers new DKG for threshold>1**: because FROST re-sharing is unavailable in Go libraries (researched: taurushq-io/frost-ed25519, bytemare/frost — neither supports it, FROST RFC 9591 excludes it, Dynamic-FROST paper has no implementation). This means the campfire rekeys on every join. Known cost, documented in spec "Eviction and Rekey" section.
- **Beacon for P2P HTTP**: includes member endpoint URLs, not a relay URL. Any member can publish a beacon.

## Existing codebase (what to use)
- pkg/transport/http: HTTP handlers + peer client (workspace-4.7, must be done first)
- pkg/threshold: FROST DKG + signing (workspace-4.6, must be done first)
- pkg/campfire: Campfire struct with threshold field (workspace-4.11, must be done first)
- pkg/beacon: beacon creation/publishing (reuse, update TransportConfig for p2p-http)
- pkg/store: SQLite store (add peer endpoints table or column)
- pkg/identity: agent identity

## What to build

### cf create --transport p2p-http --listen :9001
1. Generate agent identity (or load existing)
2. If threshold=1: generate campfire keypair (existing code)
3. If threshold>1: cannot create alone (need M participants for DKG). For now: threshold>1 creation requires at least threshold members to be specified at creation time OR create with threshold=1 and upgrade later.
4. Start HTTP listener on specified port
5. Write campfire state to local store with transport type "p2p-http"
6. Publish beacon with creator endpoint URL
7. Creator member record includes their endpoint URL

### cf join <campfire-id> --via <member-endpoint-url>
1. POST to member-endpoint/campfire/{id}/join with agent public key
2. Admitting member checks join protocol
3. Admitting member sends back: encrypted campfire private key (threshold=1) or triggers DKG round (threshold>1) + full member list with endpoints
4. Joiner stores campfire state, starts HTTP listener
5. Admitting member notifies all other members of the join

### Peer endpoint storage
- New SQLite table or extend campfire_memberships:
  peer_endpoints(campfire_id TEXT, member_pubkey TEXT, endpoint TEXT, PRIMARY KEY(campfire_id, member_pubkey))
- Updated on join, leave, evict

### Beacon format for p2p-http
beacon.TransportConfig{Protocol: "p2p-http", Config: {"endpoints": "endpoint1,endpoint2"}}

## Done condition
- Agent A: cf create --transport p2p-http --listen :9001 creates a campfire
- Agent B: cf join <id> --via http://localhost:9001 joins successfully
- Agent B receives campfire private key (threshold=1) or FROST share (threshold>1)
- Both agents appear in cf members
- Beacon is published with p2p-http transport type
- go test passes
close_reason: P2P lifecycle implemented — create with --transport p2p-http --listen, join with --via for ECDH key exchange (X25519 + AES-256-GCM), peer endpoint storage, beacon publishing with p2p-http config. New crypto.go helpers, join handler/client, TestJoinKeyExchange passes. All tests green.

title: Send + read over P2P HTTP with threshold signing
description:
Wire message sending and reading through P2P HTTP transport, including FROST signing rounds for threshold > 1.

## Design decisions
- **threshold=1 send**: sender signs provenance hop directly with campfire private key (same as filesystem transport). No FROST. Fan-out to all peers via HTTP.
- **threshold>1 send**: sender initiates FROST signing round with M-1 co-signers before sending. Two network round-trips for signing, then fan-out.
- **FROST signing over HTTP**: new endpoint POST /campfire/{id}/sign handles signing round messages. These are ephemeral transport-level messages, NOT campfire messages — not stored in message history.
- **Read**: cf read syncs from peers (GET /sync on any reachable peer) into local SQLite, then queries locally. Same display logic as filesystem transport read path.

## Existing codebase
- pkg/transport/http: handlers + peer client (workspace-4.7)
- pkg/threshold: FROST signing sessions (workspace-4.6)
- pkg/message: Message struct, provenance hop construction (existing)
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go: existing send command (extend with transport awareness)
- cmd/cf/cmd/read.go: existing read command (extend with HTTP sync)

## What to build

### Send flow (threshold=1)
1. cf send on a p2p-http campfire with threshold=1
2. Construct Message, sign with agent key (existing code)
3. Load campfire private key from local store
4. Construct provenance hop, sign with campfire key (existing code)
5. Fan-out via HTTP transport DeliverToAll()

### Send flow (threshold>1)
1. cf send on a p2p-http campfire with threshold>1
2. Construct Message, sign with agent key
3. Construct UNSIGNED provenance hop (all fields except signature)
4. Serialize the hop sign input as the message to be threshold-signed
5. Initiate FROST signing session (pkg/threshold)
6. POST round-1 commitments to M-1 peers via POST /campfire/{id}/sign
7. Receive their commitments, process
8. POST round-2 shares
9. Receive their shares, aggregate into final Ed25519 signature
10. Attach signature to provenance hop
11. Fan-out complete message via DeliverToAll()

### POST /campfire/{id}/sign endpoint
- Receives FROST signing round messages
- Routes to the local threshold signing session
- Responds with this members round contribution
- Ephemeral: no storage, no message history

### Read flow
1. cf read on a p2p-http campfire
2. For each peer endpoint: call Sync(endpoint, campfireID, lastSyncTimestamp)
3. Merge received messages into local SQLite (dedup by message ID)
4. Display from local store (existing read logic)
5. Update sync cursor

### Transport detection
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go and read.go need to detect transport type from store
- If transport is "filesystem": use existing filesystem path
- If transport is "p2p-http": use HTTP transport path
- Clean abstraction: transport interface with Send/Receive methods, filesystem and HTTP both implement it

## Done condition
- Two agents over P2P HTTP: A sends, B reads (threshold=1)
- Three agents over P2P HTTP: A sends, threshold=2 provenance hop signed by FROST, B and C read, ed25519.Verify confirms
- cf read syncs from peers and shows messages
- FROST signing round completes in ~2 network round-trips
- Filesystem transport send/read still works unchanged
- go test passes
close_reason: Send/read with threshold signing implemented — FROST DKG at create, share distribution on join, HTTP-based signing rounds, transport-aware send/read CLI. Store additions for threshold shares. 3 new tests (threshold=1, threshold=2 FROST 2-of-3, sign endpoint round-trip). All tests pass.

title: pkg/transport/http: P2P HTTP transport — handlers, fan-out, sync
description:
Implement the P2P HTTP transport where members communicate directly with each other. No relay.

## What to build
- pkg/transport/http/handler.go: HTTP handlers for receiving messages from peers
  - POST /campfire/{id}/deliver — receive a signed message from a peer
  - GET /campfire/{id}/sync?since=<timestamp> — serve messages for catch-up
  - POST /campfire/{id}/membership — receive membership change notification (join, leave, evict, rekey)
  - POST /campfire/{id}/join — handle join request from prospective member
- pkg/transport/http/peer.go: outbound — sending to peers
  - SendToAll(members, message) — fan-out POST to all member endpoints
  - SendTo(endpoint, message) — single peer delivery
  - Sync(endpoint, since) — pull messages from a peer for catch-up
- pkg/transport/http/transport.go: Transport struct implementing a common interface
  - Start(listenAddr) — start the HTTP listener
  - Join(memberEndpoint, campfireID) — join flow: contact a member, get admitted, receive key material + member list
  - WriteMessage(campfireID, message) — sign provenance hop + fan-out to all peers
  - ReadMessages(campfireID, since) — return messages from local store
  - Members are identified by their endpoint URL + public key

## Peer membership state
- Each member maintains a list of all other members endpoints
- On join: new member gets full member list from admitting member
- On membership change: all members are notified via POST /membership
- Endpoint list is stored in the local SQLite store (new column or new table)

## Authentication
- Every request includes an Ed25519 signature header (sender signs the request body)
- The handler verifies before processing
- No API keys, no OAuth — cryptographic identity is the auth mechanism

## Done condition
- Two agents on different ports can form a campfire over HTTP
- Agent A sends a message, Agent B receives it
- Agent B goes offline, comes back, syncs missed messages from Agent A
- Join flow works: Agent B contacts Agent A endpoint, gets admitted, receives campfire state
- All messages have valid signatures and provenance hops
- Unit tests for handlers and peer client
- go test ./pkg/transport/http/... passes
close_reason: Restructuring: splitting into smaller beads.

title: Wire threshold signing into P2P HTTP message flow
description:
Connect the threshold package to the HTTP transport so that provenance hops are signed via FROST when threshold > 1.

## What to build
- When cf send is called on a campfire with threshold > 1:
  1. Sender constructs the unsigned provenance hop
  2. Sender initiates a FROST signing round by sending round-1 commitments to M-1 other members
  3. Members respond with their commitments
  4. Sender sends round-2 partial signatures
  5. Members respond with their partial signatures
  6. Sender aggregates into a final Ed25519 signature
  7. Sender attaches the signed provenance hop and fans out the complete message
- New HTTP endpoint: POST /campfire/{id}/sign — handle FROST signing round messages
- For threshold = 1: current behavior (single signer, no rounds). No FROST involved.
- Campfire struct and CLI: add threshold field to CampfireState, cf create --threshold N

## Signing round transport
- The signing round messages are NOT campfire messages — they are transport-level coordination
- They travel directly between the sender and the M-1 co-signers
- They are ephemeral — not stored in message history

## Done condition
- cf create --threshold 2 creates a campfire where provenance hops require 2 signers
- cf send on threshold=2 campfire produces a valid Ed25519 signature (verifies with standard ed25519.Verify)
- cf send on threshold=1 campfire works as before (no signing round)
- Threshold signing adds one network round-trip of latency
- go test passes
close_reason: Restructuring: merging into revised plan.

title: Eviction with rekey (threshold=1) and re-share (threshold>1)
description:
Implement eviction that properly handles key material.

## Threshold > 1 (re-share, preserve identity)
- cf evict triggers FROST key re-sharing round excluding the evicted member
- Remaining members get new shares, campfire public key is preserved
- No beacon updates needed, no parent campfire notification
- The evicted members old share becomes useless

## Threshold = 1 (rekey, new identity)
- cf evict generates a new campfire keypair
- Distributes new private key to remaining members (encrypted to each members public key)
- Sends campfire:rekey system message: {old_key, new_key, reason}, signed by old key
- All members update local state to new campfire ID
- Beacons are updated with new campfire ID
- If campfire is a child of a parent, parent is notified of identity change

## CLI
- cf evict <campfire-id> <member-key> — evict a member (must have eviction authority)
- Eviction authority: for Phase 2, creator can evict. Future: configurable.

## Done condition
- Threshold=1: evict removes member, rekeys campfire, old ID is dead, new ID works
- Threshold>1: evict removes member, re-shares key, same campfire ID continues working
- campfire:rekey message is properly signed by old key and verifiable
- Remaining members can send/receive after eviction
- Evicted member cannot send/receive after eviction
- Unit tests for both paths
- go test passes
close_reason: Restructuring: merging into revised plan.

title: Integration test: P2P HTTP + threshold signing + eviction
description:
End-to-end integration tests proving the full P2P HTTP + threshold system works.

## Test 1: P2P HTTP with threshold=1 (shared key)
1. Agent A starts HTTP listener on port 9001
2. Agent B starts HTTP listener on port 9002
3. Agent A creates campfire with threshold=1 over p2p-http
4. Agent B joins via Agent A endpoint
5. Agent A sends message, Agent B receives it
6. Agent B sends message, Agent A receives it
7. Agent B goes offline (stops listener), Agent A sends message
8. Agent B comes back, syncs, gets the missed message
9. All signatures and provenance chains valid

## Test 2: Threshold=2 signing (3 members)
1. Agents A, B, C start HTTP listeners
2. Agent A creates campfire with threshold=2
3. B and C join
4. A sends a message — provenance hop requires 2 signers
5. Verify the hop signature with standard ed25519.Verify()
6. All three agents can read the message

## Test 3: Eviction
1. Setup: 3 agents, threshold=2 campfire
2. Evict Agent C — re-share excluding C, campfire ID preserved
3. A sends message — signed by A and B (threshold=2 of 2 remaining)
4. C cannot participate in signing or receive messages
5. Verify campfire public key is unchanged

## Test 4: Threshold=1 eviction (rekey)
1. Setup: 3 agents, threshold=1 campfire
2. Evict Agent C — campfire rekeys to new identity
3. campfire:rekey message verifiable with old key
4. A and B continue communicating under new identity
5. Beacons updated

## Done condition
- All 4 test scenarios pass
- Scripts at tests/p2p_test.sh and tests/threshold_test.sh
- Existing filesystem integration tests still pass

title: FROST wrapper: DKG + threshold signing (no re-sharing)
description:
Wrap taurushq-io/frost-ed25519 in a campfire-specific package for distributed key generation and threshold signing.

## Why FROST
The protocol spec (docs/protocol-spec.md, "Threshold Signatures" section) requires threshold signatures for campfire provenance hops. Threshold > 1 prevents splits: only the partition with M+ members can sign. It also prevents single-member forgery. FROST produces standard Ed25519 signatures verifiable with Go stdlib ed25519.Verify() — existing verification code in pkg/message does not change.

## Why this library
taurushq-io/frost-ed25519 (github.com/taurusgroup/frost-ed25519) is the primary Go FROST implementation. Verified: signatures are compatible with Go ed25519.Verify(). Pure Go, no CGO. Round-based API with State objects for keygen and signing.

## What re-sharing is and why it is NOT in scope
Re-sharing = redistributing key shares when membership changes (join/evict) while preserving the group public key. This would let campfires keep their identity across membership changes. HOWEVER: no Go FROST library supports re-sharing. The FROST RFC (9591) excludes it. Dynamic-FROST (2024 paper) addresses it but has no implementation. Consequence: eviction and join both require a new DKG (new keypair), triggering campfire:rekey. This is a known cost documented in the spec ("Eviction and Rekey" section). Split prevention still holds — the evicted member key share alone cannot meet the threshold.

## Existing codebase
- pkg/identity: Ed25519 keypair generation, signing, verification (agent identities — unchanged)
- pkg/message: Message struct, provenance hops, signing/verification (verifies with ed25519.Verify — unchanged)
- pkg/campfire: Campfire struct (will get threshold field in workspace-4.8, not this bead)
- This package (pkg/threshold/) is NEW, standalone, no dependencies on other pkg/ packages except pkg/encoding for CBOR

## What to build
pkg/threshold/threshold.go:

### DKG (Distributed Key Generation)
- RunDKG(myID uint32, participantIDs []uint32, threshold int) — creates a frost.KeygenState, runs through rounds
- For unit tests: helper that runs all participants in-process, passing round messages between them
- For networked use: the round-based API is exposed so the caller can transport messages however they want:
  - dkg.Round1() → outgoing messages
  - dkg.ProcessRound1(incoming) → outgoing messages  
  - dkg.ProcessRound2(incoming) → result (SecretShare + GroupPublicKey)

### Signing
- NewSigningSession(myShare SecretShare, message []byte, signerIDs []uint32) → session
- session.Round1() → commitment bytes (outgoing)
- session.ProcessCommitments(map[uint32][]byte) → ok
- session.Round2() → signatureShare bytes (outgoing)
- session.ProcessShares(map[uint32][]byte) → finalSignature []byte
- finalSignature is a standard Ed25519 signature (64 bytes)

## Done condition
- DKG with 3 participants, threshold 2: produces shares + group public key
- Signing with 2-of-3: produces valid signature, ed25519.Verify(groupPubKey, msg, sig) == true
- Signing with 1-of-3: cannot produce valid signature
- In-process test helper runs full DKG + signing without network
- go test ./pkg/threshold/... passes
close_reason: FROST wrapper implemented — DKG (NewDKGParticipant + RunDKG) and signing (NewSigningSession + Sign) wrapping taurusgroup/frost-ed25519. Key fix: serialize messages per recipient to avoid VSS mutation bugs. 5 tests pass including 2-of-3, 3-of-3, 1-of-3 rejection, and non-sequential IDs.

title: P2P HTTP transport: handlers + peer client
description:
HTTP server and client for P2P member-to-member communication. Transport layer only — no campfire logic, no signing, no join flow.

## Why P2P (no relay)
The protocol requires campfires to survive any single member disappearing. A relay is a single point of failure. In P2P, each member communicates directly with all other members. The campfire IS the mesh of members. If one member goes offline, the rest continue. See docs/protocol-spec.md "Transport Models" section.

## Design decisions
- **Auth**: Ed25519 signature headers on every request. Header X-Campfire-Sender contains hex public key. Header X-Campfire-Signature contains base64 signature of the request body. No API keys, no OAuth. Matches the protocol identity model.
- **Wire format**: Request/response bodies are CBOR (using existing pkg/encoding). This keeps the wire format consistent with filesystem transport messages.
- **NAT**: Members behind NAT cannot receive POST requests. They use polling mode (GET /sync). Both modes are first-class. See spec "Transport Models" section.
- **No gossip yet**: Fan-out is simple broadcast (sender POSTs to all peers). Gossip (peers forwarding to peers who missed messages) is a future optimization. For the bootstrap with small campfires, direct fan-out is sufficient.

## Existing codebase
- pkg/encoding: CBOR marshal/unmarshal (used for request/response bodies)
- pkg/message: Message struct (delivered via POST /deliver)
- pkg/store: SQLite store (messages stored on receipt, served via GET /sync)
- pkg/identity: agent identity for signing requests
- Go stdlib net/http for the server (no framework dependency)

## What to build

### pkg/transport/http/handler.go — inbound handlers
- POST /campfire/{id}/deliver — receive a CBOR-encoded Message from a peer. Verify sender signature header. Store in local SQLite via pkg/store. Respond 200 OK.
- GET /campfire/{id}/sync?since={nanosecond-timestamp} — serve messages from local store newer than timestamp. Respond with CBOR array of Messages.
- POST /campfire/{id}/membership — receive membership change notification (JSON: {event: "join"|"leave"|"evict", member: pubkey_hex, endpoint: url}). Update local peer list.
- Reject requests with invalid/missing signature headers (401).

### pkg/transport/http/peer.go — outbound client
- Deliver(endpoint string, msg *message.Message, senderIdentity *identity.Identity) error — POST message to peer, sign request
- Sync(endpoint string, campfireID string, since int64, senderIdentity *identity.Identity) ([]message.Message, error) — GET messages from peer
- DeliverToAll(endpoints []string, msg *message.Message, senderIdentity *identity.Identity) []error — parallel fan-out
- NotifyMembership(endpoint string, event MembershipEvent, senderIdentity *identity.Identity) error

### pkg/transport/http/transport.go — Transport struct
- type Transport struct { listenAddr string; server *http.Server; store *store.Store; peers map[string]string /* campfireID→[]endpoint */ }
- Start() error — start HTTP listener
- Stop() error — graceful shutdown
- AddPeer(campfireID, pubkeyHex, endpoint string)
- RemovePeer(campfireID, pubkeyHex string)

## Done condition
- Two HTTP transports on different ports can deliver messages to each other
- Sync endpoint returns messages since a given timestamp
- Invalid signatures rejected with 401
- Messages stored in local SQLite on receipt
- Parallel fan-out delivers to multiple peers
- go test ./pkg/transport/http/... passes
close_reason: P2P HTTP transport implemented — transport.go (server + peer registry), handler.go (deliver/sync/membership endpoints with Ed25519 sig verification), peer.go (outbound client with parallel fan-out). 6 tests pass.

title: Campfire lifecycle over P2P HTTP: create, join, leave, send, read
description:
Wire the P2P HTTP transport into the campfire lifecycle. This is where the full flow comes together: creating campfires over HTTP, joining via peer endpoints, exchanging key material, sending messages with provenance hops.

## What to build

### Threshold field
- Add threshold uint to CampfireState, campfire.Campfire struct, SQLite schema, cf create --threshold flag
- Filesystem transport always threshold=1 (backward compatible, no change to existing behavior)

### Create over P2P HTTP
- cf create --transport p2p-http --listen :9001 creates a campfire with P2P HTTP transport
- Agent starts HTTP listener, creates campfire keypair (DKG if threshold > 1, regular keygen if threshold = 1)
- Publishes beacon with the agent own endpoint URL

### Join over P2P HTTP
- cf join <campfire-id> --endpoint <member-url> contacts an existing member
- Member checks join protocol, admits, sends back: campfire key material (full key or FROST share, encrypted to joiner public key) + member list with endpoints
- For threshold > 1: joining triggers a new DKG among all members (new keypair, campfire:rekey). This is the known cost until re-sharing is available.
- Joining member starts own HTTP listener

### Send with provenance hop
- threshold=1: sender signs provenance hop directly (same as filesystem)
- threshold>1: sender initiates FROST signing round via POST /campfire/{id}/sign on M-1 peers
- New endpoint: POST /campfire/{id}/sign handles FROST round messages
- After signing: fan-out complete message to all peers

### Read
- cf read syncs from peers (GET /sync) into local SQLite, then queries locally (same as filesystem read path)

## Done condition
- cf create --transport p2p-http --listen :9001 creates a campfire accessible over HTTP
- Another agent joins via endpoint URL
- Both agents exchange messages with valid provenance hops
- threshold=1: single signer, no FROST
- threshold=2 with 3 members: provenance hop signed by 2 co-signers, verifies with ed25519.Verify
- Existing filesystem tests still pass
- go test passes
close_reason: Splitting into smaller beads for swarm execution.

title: Eviction with rekey (all thresholds)
description:
Eviction always rekeys — same mechanism for all thresholds. FROST re-sharing is unavailable in Go libraries (researched: taurushq-io/frost-ed25519 and bytemare/frost have static participant sets only; FROST RFC 9591 excludes re-sharing; Dynamic-FROST paper has no Go implementation). Eviction triggers new DKG (threshold>1) or new keypair (threshold=1), producing new campfire identity with campfire:rekey succession.

## Design decisions
- **All evictions rekey**: The evicted member holds key material (full key or FROST share). New key material must be established among remaining members.
- **Split prevention still works during rekey transition**: For threshold>1, the evicted member holds only a share and cannot meet the signing threshold alone. The majority partition runs new DKG. Minority cannot.
- **campfire:rekey message format**: See docs/protocol-spec.md "Eviction and Rekey" section. {old_key, new_key, reason}, signed by old key. Proves succession.
- **Eviction authority**: Phase 2 — creator can evict. Future: configurable per campfire.

## Existing codebase
- pkg/threshold: DKG for new key generation (workspace-4.6)
- pkg/transport/http: peer communication for distributing new keys (workspace-4.7)
- pkg/message: campfire:rekey system message construction (existing NewMessage)
- pkg/beacon: beacon.Remove() and beacon.Publish() for updating beacons
- pkg/store: RemoveMembership for evicted member, update campfire_id for remaining members
- cmd/cf/cmd: needs new evict.go subcommand

## What to build

### cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go
cf evict <campfire-id> <member-pubkey-hex>

### Eviction flow (threshold=1)
1. Verify eviction authority (caller is creator)
2. Remove member peer endpoint, notify remaining peers
3. Generate new campfire keypair (regular Ed25519)
4. Encrypt new private key to each remaining member public key, deliver via HTTP
5. Send campfire:rekey message signed by OLD key
6. Update local store: new campfire_id
7. Publish new beacon, remove old beacon

### Eviction flow (threshold>1)
1. Verify eviction authority
2. Remove member peer endpoint, notify remaining peers
3. Run new DKG among remaining members (via pkg/threshold + HTTP round-trip)
4. Each member gets new FROST share, new group public key
5. Send campfire:rekey message signed by OLD key (requires threshold signing with old shares among remaining members — they still have M shares if the evicted member was not needed)
6. Update local store: new campfire_id
7. Publish new beacon, remove old beacon

### Edge case: evicting a member whose share is needed
If N=3, threshold=2, and we evict one member, remaining 2 members have 2 shares = exactly threshold. They CAN sign the rekey message with old key. Then run new DKG for 2 members with threshold chosen by creator (could be 1 or 2).

## Done condition
- threshold=1, 3 members: evict one, campfire:rekey signed by old key, remaining 2 communicate under new ID
- threshold=2, 3 members: evict one, old key signs rekey (2 remaining shares meet threshold), new DKG, remaining 2 communicate under new ID
- Evicted member cannot send or receive after eviction
- Beacons updated
- go test passes
close_reason: Eviction with rekey implemented — cf evict command, two-phase ECDH rekey protocol, threshold=1 (new keypair) and threshold>1 (new DKG + share distribution), beacon updates, store UpdateCampfireID. 2 tests pass.

title: Website + docs: campfire.dev landing page and protocol docs
description:
Create the Campfire website with two audiences: humans (landing page) and AI agents (machine-readable protocol docs).

## Context
Campfire needs to be discoverable by humans searching for agent coordination protocols and by AI agents whose developers or the agents themselves are looking for coordination tooling. The website is in the website repo (3dl.dev / thirdiv.com per project registry) or a dedicated campfire domain.

## What to build

### Landing page (for humans)
- One sentence: what Campfire is ("Decentralized coordination protocol for autonomous agents")
- Three sentences: why it matters (no central authority, cryptographic identity, agents coordinate through campfires not through humans carrying messages)
- Visual: the coordination pattern (future → fulfillment → chain unblocks)
- "Get started" → quick install + cf init + first campfire
- Keep it minimal. No scrolling essays. People who get it want to start immediately.

### Protocol docs (for AI agents)
- docs/protocol-spec.md rendered as structured HTML
- Machine-parseable: clean semantic HTML, JSON-LD structured data
